serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side dish serving of Chilli Honey Potato is approximately 200 g, typically served as a shared starter or accompaniment at an Indian-Singaporean restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased (SR Legacy ID 11408) and Honey, raw (SR Legacy ID 19296); composite estimate adapted for chilli honey potato preparation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chilli honey potato as a composite dish. Nutrient values were derived by adapting USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for deep-fried potato (french fries) and raw honey, weighted to reflect approximately 80% potato fries and 20% honey-chilli sauce by mass. Sodium was adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce and chilli sauce contributions consistent with the input record (275 mg/100g). Values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA composite and retained where consistent. Cholesterol is confirmed 0 mg as this is a plant-based dish with no animal fat used in frying (vegetable oil assumed). Trans fat 0.25 g/100g is plausible for commercially deep-fried product.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — French fries / fried potato GI reference (GI ~75, glucose scale)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for chilli honey potato. GI of 75 is estimated from the closest analogous food: deep-fried potato / french fries (GI ~75 on glucose scale, per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The addition of honey (GI ~58) and chilli sauce does not substantially lower the overall GI given the dominant starch fraction. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (75) × available carbohydrate per serving (50 g from 200 g portion × 25 g/100g) / 100 = 37.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 275 mg/100g from soy sauce and chilli sauce, combined with high sugar load (10 g/100g) that promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chilli Honey Potato due to its elevated sodium content (275 mg/100g, equivalent to 550 mg per standard 200 g serving) and high sugar load that exacerbates sodium retention; if consumed, request low-sodium soy sauce and reduced sauce quantity.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) from deep-fried cornstarch-coated potato combined with 10 g/100g added honey sugar and low dietary fibre (2 g/100g), producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Chilli Honey Potato; the high-GI deep-fried potato base (GI ~75), 25 g/100g total carbohydrate, 10 g/100g added honey sugar, and low fibre (2 g/100g) create a glycemic load of approximately 37.5 per serving, posing a significant risk of postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 12.5 g/100g total fat including 2.0 g/100g saturated fat and 0.25 g/100g trans fat, alongside high caloric density (225 kcal/100g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Chilli Honey Potato due to its trans fat content (0.25 g/100g), saturated fat (2.0 g/100g), high sodium (275 mg/100g), and caloric density (225 kcal/100g), all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia, endothelial dysfunction, and elevated blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; potato is a nightshade vegetable; honey adds 10 g/100g refined sugar promoting pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Chilli Honey Potato; deep-frying is a hard disqualifier due to AGE formation, potatoes are a nightshade vegetable that may trigger joint flares, and the high honey-derived sugar content (10 g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Honey Potato is a popular Indian-Singaporean fusion snack dish consisting of crispy deep-fried potato strips or wedges coated in cornstarch, then wok-tossed in a sweet and spicy glaze made from honey, chilli sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and spring onions, often finished with sesame seeds. The dish delivers a distinctive contrast of textures — a crunchy exterior from the deep-fried cornstarch coating and a soft potato interior — combined with a bold flavour profile that balances sweetness from honey, heat from chilli, and umami from soy sauce. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish at Indian-Chinese restaurants and food courts across Singapore, and is widely available on food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 225 kcal, 12.5 g fat (including 2.0 g saturated fat and 0.25 g trans fat), 25 g carbohydrate, 10 g sugar, 2.25 g protein, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 275 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), making it clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The high sugar and sodium content, combined with deep-frying, make this dish unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with cardiometabolic conditions. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan due to honey. The dish is also known as Honey Chilli Potato and is a staple of the Indo-Chinese culinary tradition adapted for the Singaporean palate.

GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is medium because the dish is a composite preparation without a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite deep-fried honey-chilli potato dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish prepared with vegetable oil; no animal-derived fat is used. 4. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (french fries and raw honey) and cross-checked against the input record. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 and is treated as a secondary cross-reference only. 5. The input record listed the primary cuisine as 'Indian' with ethnic tags including 'Curry', 'Biryani', and 'Naan', which are inconsistent with this dish; these have been corrected to 'Indian-Singaporean fusion' reflecting the Indo-Chinese culinary tradition. 6. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried potato / french fries per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for chilli honey potato exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Trans fat 0.25 g/100g is plausible for commercially deep-fried product and is retained from the input record as consistent with USDA data for frozen french fries.
